item.title: The need for accountability in addressing the measles crisis. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
[Priority]

Systemic Significance

Selected by: Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, Llama-3.3-70B, Qwen-3-32B, GPT-OSS-20

The measles situation in Bangladesh reveals the shambolic state of child health management. According to a report published in the Dhaka Tribune , the test is only available at the Institute of Public Health which is currently experiencing a severe shortage of testing kits. Bangladesh has ratified major human rights treaties and was elected unanimously to serve as a Vice President of the United Nations Human Rights Council (UNHRC) in December 2024. With membership on the Council comes a responsibility to uphold high human rights standards. It has been stated in Article 25 of Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR): “Everyone has the right to a standard of living adequate for the health and well-being of himself and of his family, including food, clothing, housing and medical care and necessary social services, and the right to security in the event of unemployment, sickness, disability, widowhood, old age or other lack of livelihood in circumstances beyond his control.”. Bangladesh signed and ratified the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child (CRC) which lays the responsibility with the state: “States Parties recognize the right of the child to the enjoyment of the highest attainable standard of health and to facilities for the treatment of illness and rehabilitation of health. States Parties shall strive to ensure that no child is deprived of his or her right of access to such health care services” said in Article 24 of CRC. It also says in Article 3, paragraph 1: “In all actions concerning children, whether undertaken by public or private social welfare institutions, courts of law, administrative authorities or legislative bodies, the best interests of the child shall be a primary consideration.” If the best interest of the children were considered, availability of both vaccines and kits would have been ensured well in advance. However, authorities started acting only when the situation deteriorated. Amid the ongoing nationwide outbreak, the government has intensified its emergency vaccination campaign with the health minister recently saying that we expected to achieve full coverage soon. He claimed that infection rates and child mortality had already begun to decline following the immunisation drive and would improve further as coverage expands. Bangladesh is also committed to achieve Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) while SDG 3 is the primary goal dedicated to health, aiming to "ensure healthy lives and promote well-being for all at all ages" by 2030. The government tracks each indicator and the SDG tracker contains the findings of the Coverage Evaluation Survey 2023.  According to the CES 2023, the coverage of the first dose of the measles-rubella vaccine (MR1) dropped to 86% in 2023 from 88.6% in 2019. The decline was sharper for the second dose (MR2), falling from 89% in 2019 to 80.7% in 2023, which has raised concerns over incomplete immunisation. The decline reportedly prevented Bangladesh from achieving the herd immunity threshold of about 95%, necessary for interrupting measles transmission and meeting elimination targets. Bangladesh had aimed to eliminate measles by 2020, but the target was later extended to 2026 after repeated failures to maintain adequate vaccination coverage. The actual number of measles deaths is likely higher than the central tally, with experts speculating many deaths were not recorded as the afflicted children might not have been brought to hospitals and rather treated at home or remained untreated. The human rights principle of accountability ensures that individuals, institutions, and states are responsible for respecting, protecting, and fulfilling human rights, with mechanisms in place to provide remedies for violations. Accountability must be ensured; hundreds of children have died and continue to die and a thorough investigation is needed. It is also empirical to declare measles an epidemic and take any measures necessary to contain its spread. Oli Md Abdullah Chowdhury is a human rights worker.

item.title: The importance of efficient logistics. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
[Priority]

Systemic Significance

Selected by: Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, Llama-3.3-70B, Qwen-3-32B, GPT-OSS-20

The Dhaka Chamber of Commerce and Industry (DCCI) has rightly stressed the need for an efficient, integrated logistics system to drive Bangladesh’s economic growth. It is truly strange how, despite its undeniable importance, logistics has been treated as an afterthought in this country - fragmented, under‑resourced, and plagued by inefficiency. Yet without a modern logistics backbone, Bangladesh simply cannot hope to sustain its ambitions, be that for trade, industrialization, or global competitiveness. However, what defines our logistics is congested ports, inadequate warehousing, poor road and rail connectivity, and outdated customs procedures. Exporters face issues that erode competitiveness, while domestic businesses struggle with unreliable supply chains. Such roadblocks, when combined, not only raise costs but lead to slow delivery, eventually adding to the woes of our already-faltering business climate. The result is as expected: A dip in productivity that undermines the very progress Bangladesh seeks. An integrated logistics policy must therefore be treated as a national priority. This means streamlining coordination between ports, roads, rail, and air transport. It means investing in digital platforms and also building modern warehouses and cold storage facilities to support agriculture and industry. But, as with most of our issues, it requires governance reforms that cut red tape and ensure accountability. The government must heed DCCI’s call and put resources where they are most needed. Our nation has reached a stage where inefficiency is no longer justified. If we are serious about becoming a middle‑income nation and beyond, logistics must be built for progress, not patched together for survival.

item.title: Bangladesh is failing its children. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
[Priority]

Systemic Significance

Selected by: Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, Llama-3.3-70B, GPT-OSS-20

That, as per figures from Ain o Salish Kendra (ASK), there have been 165 children killed or raped in just the first three months of 2026 should shock us as a nation. It is important to truly recognize what this means, that behind each statistic lies a shattered family, a stolen childhood, and a nation and society that continues to be complicit in its silence. This is a crisis that simply cannot be treated as just another headline that fades with time. It is also vital to recognize that these numbers are very likely only a fraction of the real numbers in our nation, where abuse against children is oftentimes unreported, silenced, or ignored. Of course, this is also exacerbated by the absence of accountability. Time and again, outrage is expressed and promises are made to do better. Yet the cycle repeats. Perpetrators act with impunity, investigations stall, and justice is delayed or denied. This is because, as rights groups such as ASK have mentioned, many of the perpetrators were known to the victims, including teachers, neighbours and close relatives. Families, often from economically-vulnerable backgrounds, face social pressure and intimidation, discouraging them from pursuing legal action The result is that we have become a nation where violence against children has joined the list of crises that have been normalized, leading to predators being further emboldened. This disregard for child safety is systemic and the state’s failure to act decisively is nothing short of betrayal. Bangladesh must break this cycle, strengthening child protection laws and ensuring they are enforced without exception. Violence against children is intolerable, and offenders must face consequences. Bangladesh cannot look away. The killing and rape of children goes far beyond a crime and is emblematic of a moral collapse. If we fail to act, we condemn ourselves to a future where innocence continues to be perpetually at risk.

item.title: Can we tackle urban flooding without fixing how we plan our cities?. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
[Priority]

Systemic Significance

Selected by: Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, Llama-3.3-70B, Qwen-3-32B

Every monsoon, the same story unfolds: Dhaka, Chittagong, and other major cities of Bangladesh grind to a halt under the weight of severe water-logging. But the familiar images of flooded streets conceal a far more dangerous reality -- the mixing of stormwater with overwhelmed and ageing sanitation systems. When megacities flood, it is not just a matter of traffic disruption or inconvenience; it becomes a serious public health concern, exposing residents to contaminated water and increasing the risk of disease. Stagnant water increases the breeding of Aedes mosquitoes, which causes dengue. It is also a matter of damaging the products, disrupting markets, and triggering cascading economic losses across industries and households. Why does this keep happening every year? The answer lies in how our cities have been evolving. In a deltaic country like Bangladesh, the water system is the lifeline for the agriculture production, ecosystem and biodiversity, settlements and business centres, while river systems serve as the most efficient and reliable modes of transport. For cities and human settlements, these water systems also functioned as natural drainage networks, allowing excess rainwater to spread, store, and gradually recede. However, unplanned urban expansion, infrastructure and economic  development the ecological value of water systems has been steadily undermined. As a result, while water continues to define both opportunity and risk in a delta, its role in shaping where and how our cities expand has been largely overlooked. Natural drainage pathways have been disrupted, retention areas diminished, and urban development has expanded into locations that are inherently vulnerable to flooding. The outcome is a system that struggles to cope even with seasonal rainfall, making flooding not an exception, but an annual certainty. More intense rainfall events and rising river levels linked to climate change are increasing the frequency and severity of flooding. In short, big cities flooding is not just about heavy rain -- it’s also a result of how the city has grown and managed its infrastructure. Improving drainage, protecting wetlands, and better urban planning are key to reducing the problem. Is t ackling urban flooding a political p riority? One of the top political commitments of the BNP’s election manifesto (2026) was restoring and protecting Bangladesh’s natural water systems as part of its broader environmental and economic strategy. In practical terms, this includes 20,000km c anal ( khal ) re-excavation and restoration to revive natural drainage and navigation systems, protection of rivers, canals, wetlands, and other waterbodies from encroachment and illegal filling, ensuring free flow of water by removing blockages and restoring connectivity between waterways, and strengthening regulation and enforcement to prevent future degradation of water systems. This clarifies that tackling u rban f looding is one of the top priorities of the current government. The next question is how these political manifestos could be translated into development actions? Is urban flooding only an engineeri ng concern? At present, much of our response to urban flooding remains centred on engineering solutions. Drains are widened, pumps are installed, and embankments are constructed. These interventions are necessary, but they often address symptoms rather than causes. Flooding in our cities is not simply the result of inadequate drainage; it is also the outcome of how land has been used and misused over time. Canals have been filled, wetlands encroached upon, and natural retention areas converted into built-up areas. In effect, we have reduced the very spaces that once allowed cities to absorb and manage water. Improper waste disposal is one of the reasons for clogging drainages while sanitation adds another layer to this challenge. In many parts of our cities, sewerage systems will not be enough if they are not linked to drainage and land-use planning. Globally, cities facing similar challenges are beginning to adopt more adaptive planning approaches. This planning process facilitates flexible options to living with water through increasing the capacity to absorb, store, and gradually release rainwater through green spaces, permeable surfaces, and restored water bodies. Integrating this logic into modern urban planning could help reduce flood risks while improving overall urban livability. Can this be managed sustainably? The economic transformation of Bangladesh will lead to an increased rate of urbanization, and the pressure on cities will only intensify. More people, more buildings, and more infrastructure will compete for the same limited space. In a delta, this makes the question of location unavoidable, and spatial planning becomes critical. Where we allow development, how we regulate density, and whether we preserve natural water systems all determine how cities respond to rainfall. Without integrating these spatial considerations into policy decisions, investments in drainage and sanitation will continue to fall short of expectations. Equally important is the need to shift from short-term, project-based thinking to long-term, system-based planning. The Delta Plan 2100 provides a useful framework in this regard, but its success will depend on instrumenting the Spatial Planning Framework, a three-tier planning from the National, Regional, and Local level Spatial Planning, following a strong institutional framework to guide and regulate rational land use practices and to synchronize all the development actions. How can we turn this challenge into an opportunity? If we are serious about addressing urban flooding and sanitation, we also need to rethink how these sectors are financed and delivered. Historically, these have been considered as public works functions and dependent on government budgets and implemented through projectized approaches. But in reality, these are also service sectors with economic value , and they offer untapped opportunities for private investments. The challenge is not the absence of capital, but the absence of bankable, well-structured models and institutional readiness to attract the private sector investors from home and abroad. Drainage and sanitation projects are often too narrowly defined, focused on isolated engineering solutions, and disconnected from land use and urban development. As a result, potential investors see high risks but limited returns. This is where a more integrated approach can make a difference. If urban water management is planned aligning with the concepts of integrated land use, infrastructure, real estate development, new possibilities begin to emerge. Protecting flood flow zones and improving drainage, for instance, can significantly increase land values in surrounding areas. With the right policy framework, a portion of that value can be captured to finance infrastructures. Similarly, wastewater treatment and reuse can evolve into service-based business models, supplying water to industry or supporting circular economy initiatives. Ultimately, the goal is not to privatize urban services, but to mobilize all available resources from both public and private sources towards a common public good. If we can combine sound spatial planning with innovative financing and effective governance, the challenge of urban flooding and sanitation can be transformed into an opportunity for building more resilient, healthier, and better-suited to the realities of living in a delta. How can we sustain our actions? Beyond integrated urban planning, engineering and political will, effective communication serves as the vital link that turns policy into public action and contributes to sustaining the resilience. In the context of Bangladesh’s urban flooding, communication is not merely about broadcasting weather forecasts; it should connect data with community behaviour. How can we better educate our citizens that while city floods, we suffer altogether, it damages everyone’s property and affects everyone despite which economic class we belong to or where we live Baridhara or Gulshan or in Korail. The effective communication can play a powerful role  in influencing citizen’s behaviour -- helping citizens in cities like Dhaka understand how encroaching wetlands or blocking drains leads to flooding. Combined with public education, a sense of ownership among common citizens, and strong institutions and governance, communication can shift the narrative from passive victimhood to collective stewardship. This alignment is critical to sustaining initiatives under the Bangladesh Delta Plan 2100, ensuring resilience is not just planned, but practised. Achieving these demands, re-designing institutions, would be essential to ensure the livability of ourselves, for our next generation, and the generations to come. Shaikh Muhammad Mehedi Ahsan is the Principal Coordinator, Spatial Planning Affairs, and Vice President (01) of the Bangladesh Institute of Planners (BIP), working on urban resilience and infrastructure planning.
